content.text: Every call to a large language model takes time — and in agentic systems,
that time multiplies. Understanding how inference latency and serving throughput
work at the hardware and software level is essential for building agents that feel
responsive, scale under load, and remain cost-effective in production.
What it is
LLM
latency
is the elapsed wall-clock time a caller waits for a model response. It has two distinct components:
time to first token (TTFT)
— the delay before any output starts streaming — and
inter-token latency
— the gap between successive generated tokens during the decode phase.
TTFT is dominated by how long the GPU spends reading and processing the input prompt (the prefill stage).
Inter-token latency is constrained by how fast the GPU can perform a single autoregressive decode step.
LLM
throughput
is a serving-level metric: how many tokens per second the system can produce
across all concurrent users. A single request's per-token speed is related to throughput, but they are
not the same thing. A server running 64 concurrent streams at 40 tokens/s aggregate
is delivering only about 0.6 tokens/s to each caller — which feels unbearably slow even though
system-level throughput is high. Separating these concepts prevents misdiagnosis of performance problems.
In agentic pipelines the distinction matters even more because each reasoning step, each tool
invocation response, and each planning phase is a separate LLM call. Every call's TTFT and decode
time accumulates serially unless the architecture explicitly parallelises independent branches.
Why it matters
Human perception of 'fast enough' is ruthless. Research on interactive UIs puts the threshold
for 'feel instantaneous' at roughly 100 ms, 'perceptibly delayed but acceptable' at 1 s,
and 'broken or abandoned' at 10 s. A five-step ReAct agent making three tool calls per step
has 15 serial LLM round-trips. At a common production TTFT of 1.5 s per call
and 200-token responses at 40 t/s, each call takes about 6.5 s —
the total round-trip approaches 100 seconds. No user waits that long.
Beyond user experience, latency and throughput directly affect
cost
. Inference is priced per output token, but
GPU compute is priced per unit time. Low throughput means the GPU is spending more time
per token, raising effective cost. Conversely, batching to maximise throughput increases
per-user latency, creating a fundamental tension every serving system must balance.
Latency also interacts with
reliability
. Long requests are more likely to hit network timeouts, context window limits,
and token rate-limit ceilings. Shorter, faster calls compose more reliably into
robust agent loops.
Benefits of understanding latency and throughput
Knowing the mechanics of LLM inference latency unlocks several concrete improvements:
Streaming improves perceived latency
— displaying tokens as they arrive moves the UX perception point from total response time to TTFT, which is typically 5–20× shorter.
Parallelising independent calls
— replacing serial chains with concurrent fan-out collapses N×latency into 1×latency for independent subtasks.
Model routing cuts latency
— routing classification, summarisation, and routing tasks to a smaller, faster model while reserving the frontier model for reasoning can cut median latency by 60–80%.
Prompt compression reduces TTFT
— shrinking the input prompt via retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) instead of pasting full conversation history reduces prefill time proportionally to token count.
Caching eliminates repeated prefill
— prompt caching (KV-cache reuse) allows repeated system prompts or few-shot examples to be computed once and reused across requests, reducing TTFT to near zero for the cached portion.
Throughput-aware batching improves cost
— continuous batching on self-hosted GPU fleets lets the serving layer pack decode steps from multiple requests into single GPU kernel launches, dramatically raising token throughput per GPU-hour.
Trade-offs
Optimising for throughput and for latency pull in opposite directions, and every architectural
decision involves a real trade-off:
Batching vs. TTFT

: larger batch sizes improve GPU utilisation and throughput but increase the time each individual request waits in the queue. Under light load this is invisible; at saturation it causes noticeable TTFT spikes.
Speculative decoding vs. correctness guarantees

: speculative decoding uses a small 'draft' model to speculatively generate several tokens at once, verified by the main model. It can double decode speed but adds architectural complexity and fails when the draft model diverges.
Prompt compression vs. quality
: aggressively truncating context reduces TTFT but risks losing information the model needs. Retrieval quality and summarisation fidelity become critical.
Streaming vs. downstream processing
: streaming tokens to the UI improves perceived latency but makes post-processing harder — you cannot parse structured JSON incrementally without buffering logic.
Smaller model vs. capability
: routing to a cheaper, faster model saves latency and cost but may produce lower-quality outputs for complex reasoning tasks. The routing decision itself adds a small overhead call.
Self-hosted vs. API

: self-hosted GPU fleets give full control over throughput tuning and avoid per-token pricing, but require infra investment, GPU procurement, and ongoing ops. Managed APIs have unpredictable latency under their own load.
How it works
The prefill phase
When a request arrives, the model must compute the
key-value (KV) attention cache
for every token in the input prompt. This is called prefill.
Prefill is highly parallelisable across tokens — the entire sequence can be processed
in one large matrix multiplication — so it benefits from GPU tensor cores and scales
well with prompt length up to memory bandwidth limits. TTFT grows roughly linearly
with prompt token count. A 10× longer prompt ≈ 10× longer TTFT.
where
is the number of input tokens,
is the compute cost per token per prefill step (a function of model width and depth),
and
is effective floating-point throughput.
The decode phase
After prefill, the model generates one output token at a time.
Each decode step loads the full model weight tensor (many GB) from GPU HBM,
performs a single attention step over the growing KV cache, and emits one token.
The key constraint is
memory bandwidth, not compute
— the GPU is bottlenecked on reading weights, not on FLOP capacity.
This is why a single request generates tokens at only 50–80 t/s on an A100,
even though the GPU can perform hundreds of teraFLOPs per second.
For a 70B parameter model in FP16 on an 80 GB A100 (2 TB/s HBM bandwidth):
— matching observed numbers closely. Batching amortises the weight read across
multiple concurrent requests, which is why batch size is the primary throughput lever.
Serial multiplication in agent loops
Each agent step that requires an LLM call blocks until a full response is received.
Total end-to-end latency for a linear chain of
calls is:
For a five-step chain at 2 s TTFT and 100-token responses at 50 t/s:
. Tool call latency (API round-trips, database queries) adds additional wall time
that compounds this total further.
Architecture
The diagram below shows the anatomy of a single LLM request (top) and the
difference between serial and parallel agent call patterns (bottom).

attrs.alt: LLM Latency Anatomy: prefill, decode, TTFT, TPS, serial vs. parallel agent calls

content.text: In the top section, TTFT spans from the initial network arrival through queue wait and the entire
prefill phase. The decode phase then generates output tokens sequentially, each contributing to
the response. In the bottom section, serial agent calls stack multiplicatively, while parallelising
independent calls collapses total latency to approximately the slowest single call.
When to use it
Optimise for TTFT when
Building interactive chat or voice interfaces where users see tokens streaming in real time.
Running agentic loops where each LLM response gates the next tool call — reducing TTFT directly shortens the loop.
Designing reactive systems that need to start partial downstream processing before the full response arrives.
Optimise for throughput when
Running offline batch jobs: summarising thousands of documents, evaluating datasets, generating product descriptions.
Building multi-tenant platforms where many users share a self-hosted GPU fleet.
Cost-optimising background tasks that tolerate higher latency in exchange for lower per-token cost.
When not to micro-optimise
If the bottleneck is tool execution (API calls, database queries, web scraping) rather than inference, reducing TTFT has diminishing returns.
For low-QPS prototypes: optimisation overhead (speculative decoding, complex batching) often isn't worth it until you reach production scale.
When capability matters more than speed: a slower frontier model may be preferable to a fast model that gets the answer wrong.
How to implement
Step 1 — Measure before optimising
Always establish a baseline. Instrument each LLM call to capture TTFT and total response time.
Without measurements you cannot identify the actual bottleneck or verify that changes improve things.

import time
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def measured_stream(prompt: str, model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-5") -> dict:
    """Stream a completion and return latency metrics."""
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    ttft = None
    token_count = 0

    with client.messages.stream(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    ) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            if ttft is None:
                ttft = time.perf_counter() - t0
            token_count += 1  # approximate: 1 chunk ≈ 1 token

    total = time.perf_counter() - t0
    decode_time = total - ttft
    tps = token_count / decode_time if decode_time > 0 else float("inf")

    return {
        "ttft_s": round(ttft, 3),
        "total_s": round(total, 3),
        "tokens": token_count,
        "tps": round(tps, 1),
    }

metrics = measured_stream("Explain how attention works in transformers.")
print(metrics)
# e.g. {'ttft_s': 0.742, 'total_s': 4.231, 'tokens': 178, 'tps': 50.3}
Step 2 — Enable streaming to improve perceived latency
Stream tokens to the user interface as they arrive. The user begins reading while the model
is still generating, which makes the experience feel ~5× faster even when total response time is unchanged.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Anti-pattern: wait for the full response before displaying anything
# response = client.messages.create(...)
# print(response.content[0].text)  # user waits the entire generation time

# Correct pattern: stream and display incrementally
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a step-by-step plan for X."}],
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)  # tokens appear as they're generated
Step 3 — Parallelise independent calls
When two or more LLM calls do not depend on each other's output, run them concurrently
with
asyncio.gather
. This converts N×latency into approximately 1×latency for the group.

import asyncio
import anthropic

async_client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

async def call_llm(prompt: str) -> str:
    response = await async_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

async def parallel_agent_step(user_query: str) -> dict:
    # These three analyses are independent — run them in parallel
    sentiment_task = call_llm(f"Classify sentiment of: {user_query}")
    intent_task = call_llm(f"Identify intent of: {user_query}")
    entities_task = call_llm(f"Extract named entities from: {user_query}")

    sentiment, intent, entities = await asyncio.gather(
        sentiment_task, intent_task, entities_task
    )
    return {"sentiment": sentiment, "intent": intent, "entities": entities}

results = asyncio.run(parallel_agent_step("Book a flight to Tokyo next Tuesday."))
print(results)
Examples
Example 1 — Measuring and logging latency percentiles
A simple harness that runs multiple calls, collects latency samples, and reports p50/p95 statistics.
This is the foundation of any latency SLO monitoring setup.

import time, statistics, random
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def benchmark_latency(prompts: list[str], model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5") -> dict:
    ttfts = []
    totals = []
    for prompt in prompts:
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        first = True
        with client.messages.stream(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=200,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        ) as stream:
            for _ in stream.text_stream:
                if first:
                    ttfts.append(time.perf_counter() - t0)
                    first = False
        totals.append(time.perf_counter() - t0)

    def p(vals, pct): return sorted(vals)[int(len(vals) * pct / 100)]

    return {
        "n": len(prompts),
        "ttft_p50": round(p(ttfts, 50), 3),
        "ttft_p95": round(p(ttfts, 95), 3),
        "total_p50": round(p(totals, 50), 3),
        "total_p95": round(p(totals, 95), 3),
    }

# Simulate 10 varied prompts
prompts = [f"Summarise topic {i} in one sentence." for i in range(10)]
stats = benchmark_latency(prompts)
print(stats)
# e.g. {'n': 10, 'ttft_p50': 0.681, 'ttft_p95': 1.203, 'total_p50': 2.14, 'total_p95': 3.87}
Example 2 — JavaScript streaming with throughput tracking
A Node.js example using the Anthropic SDK that streams a response to the terminal
and reports TTFT and tokens-per-second after completion. Suitable as a drop-in
diagnostic wrapper for any JS/TypeScript agent.
Real-world example
Consider a customer-support agent that handles inbound tickets. For each ticket it:
(1) classifies urgency, (2) retrieves relevant policy snippets via RAG, (3) drafts a response,
and (4) generates a summary for the CRM. Steps 1, 2, and 4 are independent of each other
once the ticket text is available; only step 3 depends on step 2's retrieval results.

import asyncio
import anthropic

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

async def classify_urgency(ticket: str) -> str:
    r = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=10,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Urgent/Normal/Low? Only the label. Ticket: {ticket}"}],
    )
    return r.content[0].text.strip()

async def retrieve_policy(ticket: str) -> str:
    # Simulate a RAG lookup (real impl calls a vector DB)
    await asyncio.sleep(0.05)  # network round-trip
    return "Policy: Refunds within 30 days with receipt."

async def summarise_for_crm(ticket: str) -> str:
    r = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=60,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"One-line CRM summary: {ticket}"}],
    )
    return r.content[0].text.strip()

async def draft_response(ticket: str, policy: str) -> str:
    r = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5", max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Reply to: {ticket}\nPolicy context: {policy}"}],
    )
    return r.content[0].text.strip()

async def handle_ticket(ticket: str) -> dict:
    import time
    t0 = time.perf_counter()

    # Steps 1, 2, 4 are independent — run in parallel
    urgency, policy, crm_note = await asyncio.gather(
        classify_urgency(ticket),
        retrieve_policy(ticket),
        summarise_for_crm(ticket),
    )

    # Step 3 depends on policy (step 2's result)
    reply = await draft_response(ticket, policy)

    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    return {"urgency": urgency, "reply": reply, "crm": crm_note, "elapsed_s": round(elapsed, 2)}

ticket = "I bought a jacket 3 weeks ago and the zipper broke. Can I return it?"
result = asyncio.run(handle_ticket(ticket))
print(result)
In production this pattern reduced end-to-end ticket handling from ~12 s (all serial) to ~4 s
(parallelised), enabling 3× higher ticket throughput on the same infrastructure. The CRM summary
and urgency label — the two fastest calls — complete in parallel with policy retrieval, so the
critical-path length drops from four sequential hops to two (parallel group + draft response).
Common mistakes
Mistake 1 — Blocking on full response before continuing
Using the non-streaming API and waiting for the entire text before processing it.
This forces the user to stare at a blank screen for the full generation time.

# Wrong: waits for the entire response
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=500,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Describe photosynthesis."}],
)
print(response.content[0].text)  # user sees nothing until fully done

# Right: stream tokens as they arrive
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=500,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Describe photosynthesis."}],
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)
Mistake 2 — Running all agent calls serially when they could be parallel
Chaining
await llm_call_1()
await llm_call_2()
await llm_call_3()
in sequence when none of the calls depend on each other's output.
This multiplies latency unnecessarily.

# Wrong: serial despite no dependency
async def analyse(text: str):
    tone = await call_llm(f"Tone of: {text}")
    entities = await call_llm(f"Entities in: {text}")  # doesn't need 'tone'
    topics = await call_llm(f"Topics of: {text}")       # doesn't need either
    return tone, entities, topics

# Right: parallel
async def analyse_fast(text: str):
    tone, entities, topics = await asyncio.gather(
        call_llm(f"Tone of: {text}"),
        call_llm(f"Entities in: {text}"),
        call_llm(f"Topics of: {text}"),
    )
    return tone, entities, topics
Mistake 3 — Ignoring prompt length's effect on TTFT
Appending the entire conversation history (all previous turns) to every prompt
causes TTFT to grow linearly over the conversation length. After 20 turns with
500-token messages, the prefill input is 10,000 tokens — 10× slower TTFT
than a single-turn query.
Mitigation: compress older turns with a summarisation call, or use RAG to retrieve only
the most relevant past context. Keep the live context window to the minimum necessary.
Mistake 4 — Conflating low throughput with high latency
A model with 200 t/s aggregate throughput and 32 concurrent users delivers only 6.25 t/s
per user — the same perceived speed as a much slower model. When diagnosing slow responses,
measure per-user TPS, not system-level aggregate. A capacity shortage masquerades as a
model performance problem if you only look at aggregate metrics.
Best practices
Always stream
: enable streaming for every user-facing call. The perceived responsiveness improvement is immediate and costs nothing.
Map your dependency graph
: before wiring agent steps, explicitly identify which calls depend on prior results and which are independent. Parallelize every independent group.
Use model tiers

: reserve frontier models (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet/Opus) for tasks requiring deep reasoning. Use fast, cheap models (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Haiku) for classification, extraction, and routing — tasks with simple outputs that benefit the most from low latency.
Cap output tokens aggressively
: each token takes the same time to generate. If a task only needs a Yes/No or a 3-word category, set
max_tokens=10
rather than the default 1024.
Enable prompt caching
: prefix-cache system prompts and few-shot examples so prefill cost for the cached section is near zero on repeated requests.
Set and monitor SLOs
: define explicit SLOs for p95 TTFT and p95 total latency per call type (simple vs. complex tasks). Alert when SLOs are breached rather than waiting for user complaints.
Timeout and retry with exponential backoff
: LLM APIs occasionally stall. Set aggressive per-call timeouts (e.g., 30 s for 500-token max responses) and retry up to 3 times with jitter to avoid thundering herds.
Performance and scalability
Continuous batching (PagedAttention / vLLM)
Traditional static batching commits GPU memory for the maximum possible output length upfront,
wasting most of it.
Continuous batching
(pioneered by the vLLM project) processes decode steps from different requests together
in the same GPU kernel, evicting finished sequences immediately and inserting new ones mid-batch.
This can raise serving throughput 10–20× over naive implementations but increases
per-request TTFT slightly at high concurrency, because new arrivals may wait briefly
for a slot in the running batch.
Speculative decoding
A small 'draft' model generates K candidate tokens speculatively. The main model verifies all K
in a single forward pass. When the draft is correct (which happens with high probability for
predictable text), the main model effectively generates K tokens per forward pass instead of 1,
approaching 2–3× speedup for decode-bound workloads. The gain is model-pair and prompt-dependent;
creative or highly entropic outputs see smaller speedups.
Scaling considerations
Horizontal scaling
: replicate the model serving pod behind a load balancer. Each replica adds linearly to aggregate throughput; TTFT for any individual request depends on per-replica queue depth.
Autoscaling
: monitor queue depth and p95 TTFT. Scale GPU replicas before latency SLOs are breached, not after — cold start time on GPU instances is 60–180 s, long enough to cause sustained SLO violations.
KV cache memory
: KV cache size grows as
. For 128 K context on a 70B model, one request's KV cache exceeds 50 GB — larger than the model weights themselves. This constrains maximum concurrency.
Quantisation

: INT8 or INT4 quantisation cuts model weight size 2–4×, reducing memory bandwidth pressure and improving TPS. Quality impact is usually minor for instruction-following tasks but can be meaningful for complex reasoning.
Tooling and ecosystem
A mature set of tools exists for measuring, profiling, and optimising LLM latency and throughput:
vLLM
: open-source serving framework with continuous batching, PagedAttention, and speculative decoding. Primary tool for self-hosted throughput maximisation.
llm-perf / Hugging Face Inference Benchmarks
: standardised benchmark suite for comparing TTFT and TPS across models and hardware configurations.
LangSmith / Weights & Biases LLM tracing
: observability platforms that record per-call TTFT, total latency, and token counts in production, enabling percentile analysis and alerting.
OpenTelemetry + Prometheus
: for self-hosted setups, emit custom spans and metrics from your LLM client wrapper. Grafana dashboards can visualise TTFT histograms and queue depth over time.
Anthropic / OpenAI streaming SDKs
: both provide first-class streaming APIs in Python, TypeScript, Go, and Java, with helpers for capturing timing metadata.

# Lightweight OpenTelemetry tracing wrapper around any LLM call
from opentelemetry import trace
import time, anthropic

tracer = trace.get_tracer("llm-agent")
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def traced_llm_call(prompt: str, model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5") -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm.call") as span:
        span.set_attribute("llm.model", model)
        span.set_attribute("llm.prompt_chars", len(prompt))
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        ttft = None
        chunks = []
        with client.messages.stream(
            model=model, max_tokens=256,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
        ) as stream:
            for text in stream.text_stream:
                if ttft is None:
                    ttft = time.perf_counter() - t0
                    span.set_attribute("llm.ttft_ms", round(ttft * 1000))
                chunks.append(text)
        total = time.perf_counter() - t0
        span.set_attribute("llm.total_ms", round(total * 1000))
        return "".join(chunks)
Comparison with alternatives
Different strategies for reducing LLM call latency and improving throughput have distinct
trade-offs. The table below compares the main approaches:
Strategy
TTFT Impact
TPS Impact
Quality Impact
Implementation Cost
Streaming
Perceived 5–20× lower
None
None
Low — SDK flag
Parallelise independent calls
1× instead of N×
Unchanged per call
None
Medium — async refactor
Smaller / faster model routing
3–10× lower
3–10× higher
Risk of capability drop
Medium — routing logic
Prompt compression (RAG)
2–5× lower
Slight gain
Depends on retrieval quality
High — RAG pipeline
Prompt caching (KV reuse)
Near-zero for cached prefix
Unchanged
None
Low — SDK feature
Speculative decoding
None
2–3× higher

Minor degradation

Security and reliability
Timeout budgets and circuit breakers
Every LLM call in an agent must have an explicit timeout. Without one, a stalled or overloaded
API endpoint can block an agent thread indefinitely, consuming connections and causing cascading
failures upstream. Set a per-call timeout (e.g., 30 s for 500-token responses) and implement
a circuit breaker that opens after N consecutive timeouts, returning a graceful degraded response
rather than queuing more requests against an unresponsive endpoint.
Rate limits and backpressure
Managed LLM APIs enforce per-minute and per-day token rate limits. Paralleling many calls
simultaneously can exhaust rate limits instantly, causing 429 errors across all concurrent
requests and requiring a full retry. Apply
semaphore-based concurrency limits
to cap the number of simultaneous in-flight LLM calls, and implement exponential backoff with
jitter on 429 and 503 responses.
Latency as a reliability signal
Sudden p95 TTFT spikes often precede full API outages. Monitor latency as an early warning
signal. If p95 TTFT exceeds 2× the baseline for more than 60 seconds, consider failing over
to a secondary model or region before users experience timeouts. Latency degradation is
typically detectable 3–5 minutes before status-page incidents are published.
Key takeaways
TTFT and TPS are different metrics
: TTFT measures the prefill phase (scales with input length); TPS measures decode speed (bounded by GPU memory bandwidth, not FLOPS).
Agent latency stacks multiplicatively
: an N-step serial agent loop multiplies single-call latency by N. Map dependencies explicitly and parallelise every independent group.
Streaming is the lowest-effort win
: displaying tokens as they arrive reduces perceived latency 5–20× with a single SDK flag change, at zero cost to quality or infra.
Throughput and per-user latency oppose each other
: batching improves aggregate throughput but increases individual TTFT at high concurrency. Set SLOs for both and monitor them separately.
Model routing is often the highest-ROI optimisation
: routing simple subtasks to a small, fast model while reserving frontier models for reasoning typically cuts median latency 60–80% and reduces cost proportionally.
Prompt length directly controls TTFT
: prefill cost is linear in input tokens. Keep contexts minimal; use RAG and summarisation to prevent context windows from ballooning over long conversations.
Always set timeouts and concurrency limits
: stalled LLM calls without timeouts cause cascading failures. Semaphore-limited concurrency prevents rate-limit exhaustion when parallelising agent branches.
Measure before optimising
: instrument TTFT, total latency, and TPS per call type. Without percentile data (p50, p95), it is impossible to identify the real bottleneck or verify that changes help.
